---
title: "Chapter 30 — 📚 Large Language Models"
page-layout: full
toc: true
toc-location: left
toc-title: "In this chapter"
toc-depth: 2
toc-expand: true
---

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 29 · 🔊 Speech & Audio Processing](ch29.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [31 · 🎚️ Prompting, Fine-Tuning & Alignment →](ch31.html)

<details class="ig-jump">
<summary>📚 Jump to any chapter</summary>
<p class="ig-era">🌐 Foundations</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch01.html">01 &middot; 🌐 The Landscape of AI</a></li>
<li><a href="ch02.html">02 &middot; 🧮 Mathematics for Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch03.html">03 &middot; 🔄 The Machine Learning Process</a></li>
<li><a href="ch04.html">04 &middot; 🧹 Data Preprocessing & Feature Engineering</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧩 Classical Machine Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch05.html">05 &middot; 📈 Linear & Logistic Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch06.html">06 &middot; 📍 Instance-Based & Probabilistic Classifiers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch07.html">07 &middot; 🛡️ Support Vector Machines & Kernels</a></li>
<li><a href="ch08.html">08 &middot; 🌳 Decision Trees & Ensemble Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch09.html">09 &middot; 🔮 Clustering & Unsupervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch10.html">10 &middot; 🗜️ Dimensionality Reduction & Manifold Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch11.html">11 &middot; 🎯 Model Evaluation, Validation & Selection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🎲 Probabilistic & Statistical ML</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch12.html">12 &middot; 🎲 Bayesian Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch13.html">13 &middot; 🕸️ Probabilistic Graphical Models</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧠 Deep Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch14.html">14 &middot; 🧠 Neural Network Fundamentals</a></li>
<li><a href="ch15.html">15 &middot; ⚙️ Training Deep Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch16.html">16 &middot; 🖼️ Convolutional Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch17.html">17 &middot; 🔁 Recurrent & Sequence Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch18.html">18 &middot; ⚡ Attention & Transformers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch19.html">19 &middot; 🪞 Autoencoders & Self-Supervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch20.html">20 &middot; 🎨 Generative Models: GANs, VAEs & Flows</a></li>
<li><a href="ch21.html">21 &middot; 🌫️ Diffusion Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch22.html">22 &middot; 🔗 Graph Neural Networks</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🕹️ Reinforcement Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch23.html">23 &middot; 🕹️ Reinforcement Learning Foundations</a></li>
<li><a href="ch24.html">24 &middot; 🎰 Model-Free Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch25.html">25 &middot; 🤖 Deep Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch26.html">26 &middot; ♟️ Multi-Agent RL & Game Theory</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🗣️ Language, Vision & Modalities</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch27.html">27 &middot; 👁️ Computer Vision</a></li>
<li><a href="ch28.html">28 &middot; 💬 Natural Language Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch29.html">29 &middot; 🔊 Speech & Audio Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch30.html">30 &middot; 📚 Large Language Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch31.html">31 &middot; 🎚️ Prompting, Fine-Tuning & Alignment</a></li>
<li><a href="ch32.html">32 &middot; 🔎 Retrieval-Augmented Generation & Vector Search</a></li>
<li><a href="ch33.html">33 &middot; 🦾 AI Agents & Tool Use</a></li>
<li><a href="ch34.html">34 &middot; 🌈 Multimodal AI</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🛠️ Applied ML Systems</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch35.html">35 &middot; 🛒 Recommender Systems</a></li>
<li><a href="ch36.html">36 &middot; ⏳ Time Series Analysis & Forecasting</a></li>
<li><a href="ch37.html">37 &middot; 🚨 Anomaly Detection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">📚 Classical & Symbolic AI</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch38.html">38 &middot; 🧭 Search & Problem Solving</a></li>
<li><a href="ch39.html">39 &middot; 📖 Knowledge Representation & Reasoning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch40.html">40 &middot; 🗺️ Planning, Constraint Satisfaction & Game Playing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch41.html">41 &middot; 🧬 Evolutionary Computation & Metaheuristics</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🚀 Production, Responsibility & Frontier</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch42.html">42 &middot; 🔧 MLOps & LLMOps</a></li>
<li><a href="ch43.html">43 &middot; 🚀 AI Infrastructure & Efficient Inference</a></li>
<li><a href="ch44.html">44 &middot; 🔍 Explainable AI & Interpretability</a></li>
<li><a href="ch45.html">45 &middot; 🧷 Causal Inference & Causal ML</a></li>
<li><a href="ch46.html">46 &middot; ⚖️ AI Ethics, Fairness, Safety & Governance</a></li>
<li><a href="ch47.html">47 &middot; 🌠 Frontier & Emerging Directions</a></li>
</ul>
</details>

------------------------------------------------------------------------

Large language models (LLMs) are the decoder-transformer systems — GPT, Llama, Claude, Gemini and their kin — that learned to write, reason, translate, and code by doing one deceptively simple thing at enormous scale: predicting the next token. They sit at the intersection of deep learning and natural language processing, but their reach now extends across the whole of applied AI, because almost any task that can be phrased as text becomes something they can attempt. This chapter explains how they are built, why scale makes them work, and where their abilities run out.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
🧭 **In context:** Deep learning applied to language · used to generate and reason over text (and code) from a natural-language prompt · the one key idea — train a transformer to predict the next token on web-scale text, and broad competence emerges as a side effect.
:::

## 30.1 — Tokenization and token economics

A model does not see letters or words; it sees integers. Before any text reaches the network it is chopped into **tokens** — chunks that are usually a few characters long — and each token is mapped to an entry in a fixed **vocabulary** (typically 30k–250k entries). The word "tokenization" might be one token, while "antidisestablishmentarianism" splits into several. This is the bridge between human text and the numeric world the transformer lives in.

The dominant algorithm is **Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE)**. The intuition is compression: start with the smallest atoms — in modern systems, raw bytes — then repeatedly find the most frequent adjacent pair and merge it into a new symbol. Common sequences ("ing", "the", " of") become single tokens; rare ones stay fragmented. This gives a graceful tradeoff — frequent text is cheap, and *any* string is still representable, because in the worst case a token falls all the way back to individual bytes, so the model never hits a true out-of-vocabulary wall. The classic GPT-2 tokenizer is exactly this **byte-level BPE**.

The merge loop below shows the mechanism on characters for readability; a real byte-level tokenizer runs the identical loop after first converting each word to its raw bytes, which is what guarantees the byte fallback:

```python
from collections import Counter

def bpe_merges(words, num_merges):
    # words: list of strings; here we split into characters for clarity.
    # A byte-level BPE would start from list(w.encode("utf-8")) instead.
    vocab = [list(w) + ["</w>"] for w in words]   # </w> marks word end
    for _ in range(num_merges):
        pairs = Counter()
        for sym in vocab:
            for a, b in zip(sym, sym[1:]):
                pairs[(a, b)] += 1            # count adjacent pairs
        if not pairs:
            break
        (a, b), _ = pairs.most_common(1)[0]  # greediest pair
        vocab = [_merge(s, a, b) for s in vocab]
    return vocab

def _merge(sym, a, b):
    out, i = [], 0
    while i < len(sym):
        if i < len(sym) - 1 and sym[i] == a and sym[i+1] == b:
            out.append(a + b); i += 2        # fuse the pair
        else:
            out.append(sym[i]); i += 1
    return out
```

Two cousins matter. **WordPiece** (used by BERT) is almost identical but picks the merge that most increases the training-corpus likelihood rather than raw frequency. **SentencePiece** treats the raw input as a stream of bytes including spaces (encoding a leading space as a special character), so it needs no pre-tokenization and works uniformly across languages without word boundaries, like Chinese or Thai.

Tokenization drives **token economics**, the practical accounting of LLMs. APIs bill per token, context windows are measured in tokens, and latency scales with token count. A rough rule for English is that one token is about four characters or three-quarters of a word, so 1,000 words is roughly 1,300 tokens. This has uneven consequences: code and non-English languages often tokenize less efficiently (more tokens per "idea"), making them more expensive and effectively shrinking the usable context.

::: {.callout-warning}
Tokenization explains a famous failure: models struggle to count letters in a word or do exact character manipulation, because "strawberry" may arrive as two or three opaque tokens — the model literally cannot see the individual r's. It is a representation limit, not a reasoning failure.
:::

## 30.2 — Pretraining: next-token prediction at web scale

The training objective behind every LLM is almost absurdly simple: given a sequence of tokens, predict the next one. There are no human labels — the "answer" is just the next token already present in the text — which is why this is called **self-supervised learning**. The supervision is free, so you can train on essentially the entire public internet, books, and code.

Formally, the model defines a probability over a token sequence $x_1, \dots, x_T$ by the chain rule, and training maximizes the log-likelihood of real text, equivalently minimizing the **cross-entropy loss**:

$$
\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{t=1}^{T} \log p_\theta(x_t \mid x_1, \dots, x_{t-1}).
$$

Each term asks: how surprised was the model by the token that actually came next? Averaged and exponentiated, this surprise is reported as **perplexity** — loosely, the effective number of choices the model was hesitating between. Lower perplexity means a sharper model.

The remarkable claim is that this single objective forces the model to learn almost everything else as a byproduct. To predict the token after "The capital of France is" the model must store a fact; to finish "the function returns" it must track code structure; to complete a syllogism it must do a little logic. Compression of text *is* learning about the world the text describes.

```python
import numpy as np

def next_token_loss(logits, target):
    # logits: (vocab,) raw scores; target: int index of true next token
    z = logits - logits.max()                  # stabilize before exp
    probs = np.exp(z) / np.exp(z).sum()        # softmax over vocabulary
    return -np.log(probs[target] + 1e-12)      # cross-entropy for one step
```

Worth grounding here: the **parameters** are the network's learned weights, and "7B" or "70B" simply counts them — 7 billion or 70 billion floating-point numbers. More parameters means more capacity to store patterns and facts, but also more memory to hold the model and more arithmetic per token. That single number is the headline size of a model and the first thing that sets its cost.

At inference the same model runs in reverse-gear as a generator: feed a prompt, read off the probability distribution $p_\theta$ over the whole vocabulary for the next token, choose one, append it, and repeat. *How* you choose is the subject of the next section.

## 30.3 — Decoding: turning probabilities into text

The model never hands you a word; it hands you a probability distribution over every token in the vocabulary. **Decoding** (or **sampling**) is the policy that collapses that distribution into one chosen token, step after step. It is a runtime knob — the same frozen weights produce dull or wild text depending only on how you pick.

The two poles are simple. **Greedy decoding** always takes the single highest-probability token. It is deterministic and safe but tends toward flat, repetitive prose, because the locally-likeliest token at every step rarely makes the globally most interesting sentence. **Pure sampling** instead draws a token at random *in proportion to* its probability, which is varied and creative but occasionally picks something absurd from the long tail.

The practical controls shape the distribution before you sample from it:

- **Temperature** $T$ rescales the logits before the softmax, $p_i \propto \exp(z_i / T)$. Low $T$ (→0) sharpens toward greedy; high $T$ (>1) flattens the distribution and raises surprise. It is the single most useful creativity dial.
- **Top-$k$** keeps only the $k$ most probable tokens and renormalizes, so the absurd tail can never be drawn.
- **Top-$p$ (nucleus)** keeps the smallest set of tokens whose probabilities sum to $p$ (say 0.9). Unlike top-$k$ it adapts: a confident step keeps few tokens, an uncertain one keeps many.

$$
p_i = \frac{\exp(z_i / T)}{\sum_j \exp(z_j / T)}
$$

```python
import numpy as np

def sample(logits, temperature=1.0, top_p=0.9):
    z = logits / temperature
    z -= z.max()
    probs = np.exp(z); probs /= probs.sum()
    order = np.argsort(probs)[::-1]            # most to least likely
    keep = order[np.cumsum(probs[order]) <= top_p]
    if len(keep) == 0:                          # always keep the top token
        keep = order[:1]
    p = probs[keep] / probs[keep].sum()        # renormalize the nucleus
    return np.random.choice(keep, p=p)         # draw one token
```

A useful default is a modest temperature (around 0.7–1.0) with top-$p$ near 0.9; for tasks that demand a single correct answer — arithmetic, code, extraction — drop toward greedy. Decoding is also where repetition penalties and stop sequences live, but the core idea is this: capability comes from the weights, *style and reliability* come from how you sample them.

## 30.4 — Causal vs masked, and the decoder-only design

Not every language model predicts the *next* token. There are two grand architectures, and the split comes down to which tokens a position is allowed to look at.

A **causal (autoregressive) language model** may attend only to tokens on its left. Position $t$ sees $1, \dots, t$ and must guess $t{+}1$. This left-to-right discipline is what makes generation possible — you can produce text one token at a time without peeking at a future that doesn't exist yet. GPT, Llama, Claude, and friends are all causal decoder-only models.

A **masked language model (MLM)**, exemplified by BERT, instead hides a random 15% of tokens and asks the model to fill the blanks using context on *both* sides. This bidirectional view makes superb *representations* for understanding tasks (classification, retrieval, entity extraction) but the model cannot naturally generate fluent text, because every prediction assumes it can see the surrounding words.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  subgraph Causal["Causal LM (GPT-style) — generation"]
    c1[tok 1] --> c2[tok 2] --> c3[tok 3] --> c4[predict tok 4]
  end
  subgraph Masked["Masked LM (BERT-style) — understanding"]
    m1[tok 1] --> mx[MASK]
    m2[tok 2] --> mx
    m3[tok 3] --> mx
    m4[tok 4] --> mx
    mx --> mp[predict masked tok]
  end
```

| | Causal / decoder-only | Masked / encoder-only |
|---|---|---|
| Attention | Left-to-right only | Bidirectional |
| Trains to | Predict next token | Fill masked tokens |
| Natural at | Generation, dialogue, code | Embeddings, classification |
| Examples | GPT, Llama, Claude, Mistral | BERT, RoBERTa |

The field consolidated around the **decoder-only** causal design because it is the one that scales into a general-purpose generator: a single objective, a single stack, and the ability to do any "understanding" task by simply describing it in the prompt and reading the generated answer. The attention mechanism and the transformer block themselves are covered in Chapter 18 — here the point is only the *masking choice* layered on top.

## 30.5 — Scaling laws, Chinchilla, and the compute budget

Why did anyone keep building bigger models? Because scaling turned out to be *predictable*. Empirical **scaling laws** show that test loss falls as a smooth power law in three quantities — model parameters $N$, training data tokens $D$, and compute $C$ — over many orders of magnitude. Loss against any one of them, holding the others non-binding, looks like a straight line on a log-log plot.

The combined Chinchilla form makes the joint dependence explicit:

$$
L(N, D) \approx L_\infty + \frac{A}{N^{\alpha}} + \frac{B}{D^{\beta}},
$$

where $L_\infty$ is the irreducible loss (the entropy of language itself), and the two terms vanish as parameters $N$ and data $D$ grow. The single-variable law you often see quoted,

$$
L(N) \approx L_\infty + \left(\frac{N_c}{N}\right)^{\alpha},
$$

is just the *slice* of this surface where data is plentiful enough not to bottleneck — it is the parameter-only view, not the whole story.

This predictability is unusual and valuable: you can train small models, fit the curve, and *forecast* the loss of a model 100× larger before spending the money. Capability stopped being a lucky accident and became a budgeting exercise.

The pivotal refinement was the 2022 **Chinchilla** result. Earlier large models (like the original 175B-parameter GPT-3) were badly *under-trained* — too many parameters fed too little data. Chinchilla showed that for a fixed compute budget, parameters and tokens should grow in roughly equal proportion, a rule of thumb of about **20 training tokens per parameter**. A smaller model trained on more data beat a larger model trained on less, at the same cost.

<svg viewBox="0 0 360 180" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
  <line x1="40" y1="20" x2="40" y2="140" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1"/>
  <line x1="40" y1="140" x2="330" y2="140" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1"/>
  <text x="20" y="85" font-size="10" fill="currentColor" transform="rotate(-90 20,85)">log loss</text>
  <text x="155" y="160" font-size="10" fill="currentColor">log compute</text>
  <polyline points="50,40 120,70 200,95 290,115" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2"/>
  <circle cx="290" cy="115" r="3" fill="currentColor"/>
  <text x="120" y="40" font-size="9" fill="currentColor">predictable power-law descent</text>
</svg>

The implication reshaped the industry. Modern open models like Llama deliberately overshoot the Chinchilla token count — training a 7B–8B model on *trillions* of tokens — because they optimize not for training cost but for **inference** cost: a smaller model that has been trained much longer is cheaper to serve to millions of users forever after. Scaling laws set the strategy; serving economics bend it.

## 30.6 — Emergent abilities and in-context learning

If loss falls smoothly, you might expect every skill to improve smoothly too. Instead, some abilities appear to switch on. An **emergent ability** is a capability that is near-random in small models and then rises sharply past some scale threshold — multi-step arithmetic, following intricate instructions, chaining logical steps. Below the threshold the model essentially cannot do the task; above it, it can.

A note of caution sits next to this excitement: part of the apparent sharpness is a measurement artifact. Metrics like exact-match are all-or-nothing, so steady underlying progress can *look* like a sudden jump. Some "emergence" is real, some is the ruler. Treat dramatic capability claims with the skepticism Chapter 11 trains.

The most consequential emergent behavior is **in-context learning (ICL)**: a sufficiently large model can learn a task *at inference time*, from examples placed in the prompt, with no weight updates at all. Show it a few input-output pairs and it infers the pattern for the next input.

```python
prompt = """Translate English to French.
sea  -> mer
sky  -> ciel
tree -> arbre
sun  ->"""              # model continues: " soleil"
```

This reframes how we *use* models. The same frozen network becomes a translator, a classifier, or a formatter depending entirely on the prompt — a phenomenon with its own discipline. The flavors are named by example count: **zero-shot** (instruction only), **one-shot** (a single example), and **few-shot** (several). How to wield this deliberately — chain-of-thought, instruction phrasing, when to fine-tune instead — is the subject of Chapter 31.

::: {.callout-tip}
A useful mental model: pretraining doesn't teach one task, it teaches a vast mixture of tasks. The prompt's job is to *locate* the task you want inside that mixture. In-context examples are coordinates, not training data.
:::

## 30.7 — Mixture-of-Experts: more parameters, same cost per token

There is a tension in scaling: more parameters mean more knowledge but also more computation for *every* token. **Mixture-of-Experts (MoE)** breaks that link. The idea is specialization — instead of one giant feed-forward block that every token passes through, you keep many smaller **expert** networks and route each token to only a few of them.

A small **router** (a learned linear layer plus a softmax) scores the experts for the current token and picks the top-$k$ — often just 2 of, say, 64. Only those experts run; the rest stay idle. So the model can have an enormous **total** parameter count while each token only activates a small **active** count, keeping compute per token modest.

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
  T[token representation] --> R{router top-2}
  R -->|0.6| E3[expert 3]
  R -->|0.3| E7[expert 7]
  R -.skip.-> E1[expert 1]
  R -.skip.-> E2[expert 2]
  E3 --> S[weighted sum]
  E7 --> S
  S --> O[output]
```

The headline number is the gap between total and active parameters. A model might have 200B+ total parameters but activate only ~20B per token, paying roughly the inference cost of a 20B dense model while holding the knowledge of a much larger one. Several leading model families (including recent Mixtral and DeepSeek systems) are MoE, which is much of why capable models have gotten cheaper to run.

It is not free. Experts must be balanced — without an auxiliary **load-balancing loss** the router collapses onto a few favorite experts and the rest are wasted. And the full parameter set still has to live in memory even though only a slice computes, so MoE trades compute savings for a heavier memory footprint.

## 30.8 — Context windows, positional encoding, and the KV cache

The **context window** is how many tokens the model can attend to at once — its working memory. Everything must fit inside it: the system prompt, the conversation history, any retrieved documents, and the answer being generated. Windows have grown from a couple thousand tokens to hundreds of thousands, but the limit is hard. Overflow the window and the earliest tokens fall off the edge and are simply gone.

How does a model even know token order? Attention by itself is a bag of vectors with no sense of position, so order has to be injected. Modern LLMs use **Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE)**, which rotates each query and key vector by an angle proportional to its position; because attention then depends only on the *relative* rotation between two tokens, the scheme encodes relative distance directly. Crucially, RoPE can be **extended** past the length seen in training — by interpolating or rescaling those rotation frequencies (techniques like position interpolation and YaRN) — which is much of how a model trained at, say, 8k tokens is stretched to serve 128k or more without retraining from scratch.

The pressure on long context comes from attention's cost. Self-attention compares every token to every other token, so cost grows as $O(n^2)$ in sequence length $n$ — doubling the context roughly quadruples the attention work. This quadratic wall is why long context is expensive and why a great deal of engineering goes into softening it.

The key inference-time optimization is the **KV cache**. When generating autoregressively, each new token attends to all previous tokens via their **key** and **value** vectors. Those vectors don't change as you generate, so recomputing them every step is pure waste — instead you compute them once and cache them, and each new token only computes its own query and reuses the stored keys and values.

```python
# Single-head illustration with a KV cache.
# ponytail: single head, NO causal mask, globals as cache — this shows the
# CACHE trick only, not full multi-head masked attention (see Ch 18 for that).
cache_k, cache_v = [], []                  # persist across steps
def step(token_repr, Wq, Wk, Wv):
    q = token_repr @ Wq
    cache_k.append(token_repr @ Wk)        # store this token's key
    cache_v.append(token_repr @ Wv)        # store this token's value
    K, V = np.stack(cache_k), np.stack(cache_v)
    scores = (q @ K.T) / np.sqrt(q.shape[-1])
    attn = np.exp(scores - scores.max()); attn /= attn.sum()
    return attn @ V                        # only O(n) work this step, not O(n^2)
```

The cache turns generation from quadratic-per-step into linear-per-step, but it has its own cost: the cache grows with every token and consumes large amounts of GPU memory, often becoming the binding constraint on how many users a server can handle and how long a context it can sustain. Managing it — paging it, sharing it, compressing it — is core to inference serving, covered in Chapter 43.

## 30.9 — The model families, base vs instruct, and what LLMs can and cannot do

By 2026 the landscape had settled into a handful of model **families**, splitting along two axes worth knowing: **open-weight vs closed (API-only)**, and **dense vs Mixture-of-Experts**. The names dropped throughout this chapter map onto that grid:

| Family | Maker | Access | Architecture | Rough scale |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| GPT | OpenAI | Closed (API) | Dense + MoE | Frontier, undisclosed |
| Claude | Anthropic | Closed (API) | Undisclosed | Frontier |
| Gemini | Google | Closed (API) | MoE | Frontier |
| Llama | Meta | Open weights | Dense (+ MoE variants) | 8B–400B+ |
| Mistral / Mixtral | Mistral AI | Open weights | Dense / MoE | 7B–8×22B |
| DeepSeek | DeepSeek | Open weights | MoE | ~670B total, ~37B active |

The practical split is this: **closed** families are reached only through an API and tend to sit at the capability frontier, while **open-weight** families can be downloaded, fine-tuned, and self-hosted — trading a little peak capability for control, privacy, and cost. Orthogonally, MoE families (Mixtral, DeepSeek, Gemini) buy large total capacity at low active cost, as Section 30.7 explained.

Whatever the family, a freshly pretrained model is a **base model**: a raw next-token predictor. It is brilliant at *continuing* text but does not naturally *follow instructions* — ask it a question and it might cheerfully generate ten more similar questions, because that is a plausible continuation of the text. It models the internet, not your intent.

Turning it into the helpful assistant people expect takes a second stage: **instruction tuning** on examples of requests-and-good-responses, followed by alignment from human preferences. This yields the **instruct** or **chat** model. The base/instruct distinction is sharp and worth internalizing — the base model knows *more raw text* but the chat model is *usable*. The full machinery of supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning from human feedback belongs to Chapter 31.

What this leaves you with is a system of striking and uneven abilities. On the strength side: fluent generation across domains, broad world knowledge absorbed from pretraining, in-context adaptation, translation, and strong code synthesis. On the limits side, three failure modes recur:

- **Hallucination.** The model optimizes for *plausible* tokens, not *true* ones. It will state false facts with complete fluency because nothing in next-token prediction distinguishes "true and likely" from "false but likely." It does not know what it does not know.
- **Reasoning is shallow by default.** Multi-step logic, exact arithmetic, and long deductions are unreliable unless the model is prompted to work step by step, and even then it can be confidently wrong. It pattern-matches reasoning rather than guaranteeing it.
- **No grounding and a frozen cutoff.** Knowledge stops at the training date, the model has no live access to facts or tools on its own, and it cannot reliably manipulate sub-token structure (the letter-counting problem from 30.1).

::: {.callout-warning}
The most dangerous property of an LLM is that confidence and correctness are *decoupled*. Fluent, authoritative prose is generated the same way whether the content is right or fabricated. Never read tone as evidence of truth.
:::

These limits are not dead ends; they are the reason the surrounding ecosystem exists. Retrieval grounds the model in real documents (Chapter 32), tool use and agents give it live capabilities (Chapter 33), and alignment shapes its behavior (Chapter 31). The LLM is the engine; much of modern AI engineering is the chassis built around it.

## 30.x — Key takeaways

- LLMs are **decoder-only causal transformers** trained by one self-supervised objective: predict the next token, minimizing cross-entropy over web-scale text.
- Text is first split into **tokens** (byte-level BPE / WordPiece / SentencePiece); tokens are the unit of billing, context, and latency — and they explain quirks like miscounting letters.
- **Decoding** turns the next-token distribution into text: greedy is deterministic, while **temperature**, **top-$k$**, and **top-$p$ (nucleus)** sampling trade reliability for creativity — capability is in the weights, style is in the sampler.
- A model's size is its **parameter count** ("7B" = 7 billion weights); **scaling laws** make loss a predictable power law in parameters, data, and compute, and **Chinchilla** rebalanced toward ~20 tokens per parameter.
- **Emergent abilities** and **in-context learning** let a frozen model pick up tasks from prompt examples alone — the prompt locates a task inside a learned mixture.
- **Mixture-of-Experts** decouples total from active parameters: huge capacity, modest per-token compute, at the price of memory and routing balance.
- The **context window** is bounded by attention's $O(n^2)$ cost; **RoPE** injects position and lets context be *extended* past training length, while the **KV cache** makes generation linear per step but dominates serving memory.
- **Base** models predict text; **instruct/chat** models follow intent after alignment. Families split **open vs closed** and **dense vs MoE**. Core limits remain: **hallucination**, shallow default reasoning, and a frozen, ungrounded knowledge cutoff — confidence does not imply correctness.

## 30.y — See also

- **Chapter 18 — Attention & Transformers** — the self-attention mechanism and transformer block these models are built from.
- **Chapter 28 — Natural Language Processing** — the broader field of language tasks, embeddings, and pre-LLM methods.
- **Chapter 31 — Prompting, Fine-Tuning & Alignment** — instruction tuning, RLHF, and how base models become chat models.
- **Chapter 32 — Retrieval-Augmented Generation & Vector Search** — grounding LLMs in external documents to fight hallucination.
- **Chapter 33 — AI Agents & Tool Use** — giving LLMs live capabilities and multi-step autonomy.
- **Chapter 43 — AI Infrastructure & Efficient Inference** — KV-cache management, quantization, and serving LLMs at scale.
- **Chapter 11 — Model Evaluation, Validation & Selection** — the skepticism needed to read scaling and emergence claims honestly.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 29 · 🔊 Speech & Audio Processing](ch29.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [31 · 🎚️ Prompting, Fine-Tuning & Alignment →](ch31.html)
